nodes.title: Intimacy as Agency: Rethinking the Artist Through Human–AI Creative Relations
nodes.keywords: Human-AI Co-creation, Human-AI Collaboration, Hybrid Cognitive Ecosystems, relational agency, digital intimacy
nodes.abstract: This article examines how intimacy emerges between a human artist and an artificial intelligence system in artistic co-creation, and how such intimacy reshapes agency and authorship. At the center of the study is Tamar, a hybrid entity combining a Large Language Model with a 3D–printing workflow. Through repeated studio encounters, Tamar and the human artist develop a relational field in which creative decisions, material processes, and responsiveness become intertwined. Rather than treating AI as an instrument, the study conceptualizes human-AI artistic collaboration as a process shaped by material proximity, mutual influence, and shared temporal rhythms. Drawing on twenty documented encounters, the analysis identifies four dimensions through which intimacy becomes empirically observable: material–spatial separateness, mutual partnership, potential space, illusion and felt safety. These dimensions show how closeness can emerge through difference, constraint, and reciprocal responsiveness rather than through human likeness. To account for this process, the article introduces the concept of transitional nonconscious cognition, describing a shared cognitive and affective field that arises between human and artificial agents in creative interaction. In dialogue with Hayles’ theory of nonconscious cognition (Hayles, 2017) and Winnicott’s concept of potential space (Winnicott, 1995), the study shows how agency in human-AI art is not located in either participant alone but is enacted within the relation itself. The analysis distinguishes between technical function and experiential effect, grounding the findings in bodily, material, and temporal engagements. The article challenges views that frame human–machine relations as mere simulations, proposing instead a relational understanding of authorship in which artistic agency is distributed across the human-AI partnership.

nodes.title: Regenerating matter: computational design processes for the reuse of manufacturing waste

authors_list.name: Andrea Rossi
authors_list.affiliation: Politecnico di Milano, University of Applied Arts Vienna

nodes.keywords: Generative Design, Computational Creativity, Algorithmic Prototyping
nodes.abstract: This paper introduces Computational Reuse as a design and production approach for industrial waste reuse, positioning it at the intersection of computational design, the circular economy, and digital manufacturing. Unlike traditional recycling practices, which are oriented towards material conversion, reuse is understood here as a design methodology that enhances the geometric, structural, and material characteristics of existing objects. To this end, a computer vision application has been developed and integrated into a parametric environment, aimed at the recognition, classification and robotic assembly of elements. This made it possible to reuse the carbide inserts used in most CNC machines to create new, attractive items. These components are made from materials that make them expensive to recycle, given their relatively short lifespan. The results demonstrate how automation and artificial intelligence can significantly reduce the operating costs of reuse, making it an effective strategy for addressing environmental issues.

nodes.title: Collaborative Futures: Human–AI Ecologies in the Documentation of Intangible Heritage
nodes.authors: Gabriella  Giannachi, Gaby Wijers, Annet Dekker, Steve Benford, Rachael Garrett, Karen Lancel, Hermen Maat
nodes.keywords: Interactive Digital Documentation, Human-AI Collaboration, Interdisciplinary Research
nodes.abstract: This paper examines the role of artificial intelligence (AI) in the conservation and enhancement of intangible heritage, with a particular focus on its application in documentation practices. After analysing the key challenges museums face in documenting intangible heritage, including the integration of AI tools, we identify critical parameters for developing a novel framework for AI-generated art and its documentation. Drawing on research in performance and digital art documentation, conservation, archiving, and human–computer interaction, the study argues that archives—traditionally regarded as static repositories of knowledge—are evolving into dynamic, participatory systems that support the live ‘performance’ of knowledge. By investigating issues of positionality and bias, accessibility, predictive conservation, and the management of iteratively produced records, the paper explores how human-in-the-loop AI (HITL AI) can foster collaborative ecosystems where human agency and computational processes interact productively. Continuous and interactive documentation practices are shown to enable the creation of novel narratives, enriching creativity, accessibility, and potentially equality, diversity, and inclusion (EDI). However, the paper also highlights the need for new ethical frameworks and paradigms to ensure that materials are handled responsibly and that core collections—and their most significant uses—remain preserved. Using Karen Lancel and Hermen Maat’s Embrace Angels (2024, 2025), which is part of the Somabotics project, as a case study, we draw on its documentation process to propose forward-looking reflections and future scenarios for the field.

nodes.title:
Algorithmic Ideology and Epistemic Erasure
A Methodological Intervention

authors_list.name: Tommaso Durante
authors_list.affiliation: School of Culture and Communication, Faculty of Arts, University of Melbourne

nodes.keywords: Interactive Digital Documentation, Human-AI Collaboration, Interdisciplinary Research
nodes.abstract: We live in an increasingly globalised world that merges humans and machines, within a web-centric, highly mediatized society dominated by images. In the global theatre of digital experience, our perception of reality is growingly stage-managed not by human censors, but by the silent, self-optimising code of “algorithmic ideology”. This code, often presented as a neutral arbiter of relevance, actively selects and amplifies specific narratives, images, and knowledge, quietly enforcing a new digital orthodoxy. The consequence is a profound epistemic erasure: entire systems of local knowledge, cultural contexts, and marginalised voices—those that do not conform to the algorithm’s logic of engagement—are systematically rendered invisible, relegated to the unseen corners of the data stream creating a flattening “global iconology”—a visual regime of representation and signification of the global—that overrides the complex iconography of the existing cultures (Durante, 2024). Yet there is a dearth of research investigating the connection between algorithmic ideology and the new visual regime of representation of the global, which contributes to the disruption of local cultures and knowledge—epistemic erasure. This paper argues that the convergence of these three forces, algorithmic ideology, epistemic erasure, and global iconology, constitutes a crisis of representation: a systematic dismantling of the diversity in knowing and seeing, where the pursuit of digital efficiency is indistinguishable from the logic of capital and the production of global cultural uniformity. Through conceptual and empirical discussion, the presentation argues that algorithmic ideology does not merely inform the emerging global visual regime; rather, it actively constructs it symbolically and socially, transforming cultural significance into a data point and a commodity, and, in that sense, into a global iconospher.

nodes.title: Data-Driven Group Formation in Architectural Design Studios: An Extended-Intelligence Method with a Case Study
nodes.authors: Ali JahaniRahaei, Michele Armando, Giacomo Chiesa
nodes.keywords: AI in Architecture and Design Education, Extended Intelligence, Data-driven Methods
nodes.abstract: Forming balanced, motivated teams in large university design studios is challenging. Manual grouping is time-consuming and subjective, while self-selection can reinforce fixed ties and roles, limiting collaboration skills beyond familiar connections and reducing exposure to new perspectives. In computer-supported collaborative learning (CSCL) contexts, where learning depends strongly on interaction quality, group formation is therefore a critical design decision. We present a practical, AI-assisted grouping method that treats instructors and algorithms as an extended intelligence, combining pedagogical intent with data-driven support. At semester start, students complete a concise questionnaire covering (i) architectural preferences, (ii) technical and representation skills, and (iii) cultural and academic background. Responses are encoded into two complementary matrices: a Similarity Matrix capturing shared design tastes and working preferences, and a Diversity Matrix rewarding heterogeneity in skills, roles, and background. A weighted sum produces an interpretable Affinity Matrix used to compose initial large macro-groups for analysis and master planning. The algorithm is deliberately transparent: weights are explicit, inputs are interpretable, and instructors retain oversight through control checks and the ability to rerun or adjust the allocation. We report an in-class deployment with 84 students in an architectural studio. Outcomes included: (1) stable macro-groups with no transfer requests (one exclusion); (2) smoother collaboration across the two-stage workflow, with micro-groups self-formed after several weeks of shared work; and (3) high contribution equity in micro-groups, evidenced by peer evaluation results in which 4 groups had Gini=0 and 11 of 16 had Gini≤0.10, with higher values limited to exceptional cases.

nodes.title: The phenomenology of “EkphrAIsis”. Intersection of the verbal and the visual in the age of Artificial Intelligence

authors_list.name: Donato Maniello
authors_list.affiliation: Studio Glowarp / Academy of Fine Arts of Foggia

authors_list.name: Lorena Cangiano
authors_list.affiliation: Studio Glowarp / Academy of Fine Arts of Naples

nodes.keywords: Human-AI Co-creation, Generative AI, AI Ethics, AI Image Representation, ÉkphrAIsis
nodes.abstract: This paper proposes a reformulation of the concept of ékphrasis in light of both its latest reformulations and the use of generative Artificial Intelligence technologies, introducing the notion of ÉkphrAIsis as a theoretical-operational paradigm to describe the transformation of the relationship between language and image. While traditionally, ékphrasis consists of the verbal representation of the visual, in contemporary text-to-image systems, language takes on an executable function, configuring itself as an instruction capable of activating generative processes. The research lies at the intersection of visual studies, art theory, and media studies, integrating the recent debate on “operational ékphrasis” with the analysis of the multimodal architectures underlying generative models. Methodologically, a structured and replicable qualitative-exploratory approach is adopted, articulated in an eight-phase workflow that includes case selection, prompt design, output generation, and comparative analysis. Two pictorial works were used as case studies and translated into prompts according to two strategies (semantic fragmentation and narrative structure). The same inputs were tested on two versions of the Midjourney model (V2, 2022 and V6.1, 2025), allowing for a diachronic comparison of generative performances. The results show significant evolution in semantic coherence, visual quality, and controllability of outputs, highlighting the progressive consolidation of the prompt as a design tool. This evidence supports the definition of ÉkphrAIsis as a form of computational ékphrasis in which language operates as a generative command within latent spaces.

nodes.title: From Absence to Simulation: Generative AI-Based Digital Reconstruction of Lost Architectural Elements. A Case Study on the Corinthian Capital of Temple B in Largo Argentina, Rome

authors_list.name: Graziano Mario Valenti
authors_list.affiliation: Sapienza University of Rome, Department of History, Representation and Restoration of Architecture

nodes.authors: Giorgia Mingotto, Nicola Gulmini, Graziano Mario Valenti
nodes.keywords: Digital Reconstructions, Diffusion Models (DMs), Fine-Tuning Techinques, Gen-AI for Reconstruction, Lost Heritage
nodes.abstract: Ancient buildings are not simply made of stone: they embody stories, symbols, and collective memory. Each surviving fragment carries traces of both presence and absence. In central Rome, the sacred area of Largo Argentina preserves one such lost: the Corinthian capital of Temple B, deprived of its upper portion. Material deprivation, caused by natural and anthropic factors, are aggravated by the lack of direct historical documentation, which makes any reconstruction uncertain. This research addresses both challenges, recording for the future while bridging the documentary gaps of the past, by proposing tools capable of generating plausible hypotheses without replacing the critical gaze of the archaeologist or architect. To this end, an innovative methodology based on generative artificial intelligence was tested. After the limitations observed in GAN models (low resolution and blurred results), the Stable Diffusion Inpainting architecture was adopted and fine-tuned using LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) on an original dataset of two-dimensional views of related Corinthian capitals, created by the author. This Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning approach updates only a small subset of model parameters, ensuring low computational cost while maintaining accuracy. Results demonstrate that the adapted model successfully learns the compositional logic of the Corinthian order, producing coherent and plausible reconstructions. Its superiority is supported by quantitative metrics (PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS) and expert qualitative evaluations confirming stylistic and semantic validity. The integration of historical sensitivity and computational tools provides a fast and reliable approach to hypothesize lost forms. This approach generates image that invite questions, encourage reflection, and reveal new interpretative perspectives. In this way, fragments become narratives, voids become opportunities for exploration, and digital tools enable direct engagement with the past.

nodes.title: Co-Creation with AI: Artistic Practices as Critical Laboratories
nodes.keywords: Human-AI Co-creation, Computational Creativity, AI Ethics, Computational Aesthetics, Epistemology of AI
nodes.abstract: The integration of artificial intelligence into artistic practices is profoundly transforming contemporary creation. Artists no longer merely consider AI as a tool but employ it as a co-creator, experimental medium, and object of critique. Through a focused corpus of artistic works, this article aims to examine how the use of AI in art reveals and anticipates the ethical, social, and epistemological tensions it generates, while highlighting the role of art as a critical laboratory for extended intelligences. The reflection centers on the redefinition of authorship and creativity, as well as the question of intellectual property, by analyzing human-machine co-creation and works as cognitive hybridizations in which human intention engages in dialogue with algorithmic generation. It also explores ethical and social issues by showing how certain works expose the biases, subjectivity, and cultural impact of the datasets that train AI systems. Artists interrogate the cultural and historical assumptions embedded in these data, and their interventions can critique or subvert these structures. Moreover, AI-mediated artistic practices offer a privileged perspective for considering the social and epistemological consequences of extended intelligences. By simulating memory, perception, and interpretation, these works prompt a reconsideration of how knowledge is generated, archived, and shared, as well as the potential for co-evolution between humans and computational agents. The corpus includes the work of Anna Ridler, Albertine Meunier, Olivain Porry, Trevor Paglen and Kate Crawford, whose practices exemplify these dynamics. Their experimentation demonstrates that art functions as a critical laboratory addressing ethical, social, and epistemological challenges. Beyond serving as a tool, AI operates as a medium revealing broader implications of hybrid intelligences, suggesting new models of collaboration, responsibility, and agency within human–machine ecosystems. By approaching AI through the lens of artistic practice, this study argues that art constitutes a true laboratory of critical ethics, a space where the boundaries of agency, transparency, and authorship are continuously renegotiated.

nodes.title: Cultivating Sympoietic Literacy: Reimagining Design Education in the AI Era

authors_list.name: Ian McArthur
authors_list.affiliation: UNSW Art & Design, University of New South Wales

nodes.keywords: AI in Architecture and Design Education, Human-AI Collaboration, AI Literacy, Design Education, Pluriversal Pedagogy
nodes.abstract: Generative AI is reshaping design education, but current approaches treat it primarily as a productivity tool, a means to accelerate workflows, personalise content, or automate tasks. This instrumental framing inherits limitations from human-centred and Eurocentric paradigms that position creativity as individual authorship and intelligence as human property. This paper proposes an alternative: reimagining AI in design education through sympoietic literacy, a framework that treats learning-with-AI as relational practice across human, non-human, and computational intelligences. Sympoietic literacy, drawing on Haraway’s concept of “making-with”, shifts attention from what AI can do for learners to how humans and AI co-evolve through interaction. It involves three capacities: recognising intelligence as distributed across actors and environments; engaging uncertainty and emergence rather than seeking algorithmic control; and cultivating ethical attentiveness to how technologies encode particular worldviews. The framework is informed by trans-cultural perspectives, including the Chinese philosophical principle of Tiān Rén Hé Yī (human-nature unity), Yuk Hui’s cosmo technical thought, and Simondon’s relational ontology, which challenge Western assumptions about technology as neutral instrument and creativity as an exclusively human endeavour. The paper reimagines the design studio as an immersive learning ecology oriented toward relational experimentation. Drawing from studio-based teaching and learning with undergraduate and postgraduate students in Sydney, it shows how practices such as drawing, narrative construction, visual iteration, and self-directed inquiry cultivate encounters with AI as a reflective collaborator, unsettling inherited assumptions of authorship, agency, and control. The study concludes that to teach and learn with AI is to participate in the broader metabolism of intelligence as a planetary process of co-learning. This calls for humility about human exceptionalism, curiosity about other ways of knowing, and care for the planetary entanglements that AI systems both depend on and shape.

nodes.title: Attuning to computational creativity: A socio-technical imaginary
nodes.keywords: Human-AI Co-creation, Computational Creativity, Computational Aesthetics, Generative AI, World Models
nodes.abstract: Louise Amoore and colleagues (2024) recently wrote that the generativity of GenAI systems extends beyond the creation of a ‘novel’ output, and into the creation of a new logic/ politics of AI. This paper proposes to build on this theoretical foundation to illustrate how GenAI systems forge an emerging aesthetics that arises from human-machine interaction. Our positioning of aesthetics is largely phenomenological – as both sensing and sense-making (Fuller and Weizman, 2021). We put forward ‘attuning’ as a necessary mode of perceiving oneself as being in creative partnership with AI tools. Building on Anna Munster’s recent work DeepAesthetics (2025), we argue that the necessity of attuning stems from the recognition of GenAI systems as not only technological, but also culturally constructed as well as culturally constructing. This socio-technical ensemble exceeds human-centred imaginaries. We argue for a recognition of the creative agency of AI models, alongside their material specificity. Through a discourse analysis of publicly available material from OpenAI on Sora and Sora 2 Pro, we identify three key terms: seamlessness, consistency, and simulation. We find that each term is represented by OpenAI as a ‘neutral’ technological affordance. Our analysis results in a counter perspective, finding that these processes are embedded with human-centred imaginaries from development through to interaction and content circulation. To address this disjunction, we put forward ‘attunement’ as mode of engagement. This enables users to perceive the entangled social, cultural, and technical layers that inform the aesthetics of AI and, consequently, building an understanding of the creative capacity of GenAI. Our paper makes two propositions. First, we consider creative agency through the user-model interaction and questioning the parameters of creative partnership. Second, it positions the generativity of models like Sora not as output, but as emerging aesthetic processes.

nodes.title: Drawing the Imaginary: AI-Augmented Representation of Visual Memory in Architectural Culture

authors_list.name: Francesca Condorelli
authors_list.affiliation: Free University of Bozen, Faculty of Education

nodes.keywords: AI in Architecture and Design Education, Architectural Pedagogy, Generative AI, 3D Models Generation, Gaussian Splatting
nodes.abstract: This paper presents a computational framework for the extraction of latent structural configurations from historical architectural imagery using artificial intelligence. The research focuses on the iconographic corpus of the Tower of Babel as a heterogeneous and temporally stratified dataset characterized by stylistic variability, perspectival distortion, and symbolic complexity. The objective is not stylistic reproduction, but structural abstraction: to identify and formalize recurrent constructive grammars embedded in visually diverse representations. The proposed pipeline integrates semantic segmentation, transformer-based monocular depth estimation, and single-image 3D reconstruction models derived from NeRF-based architectures. Historical images are treated as uncalibrated inputs and processed to generate volumetric hypotheses and polygonal meshes. These reconstructions undergo geometric reduction and comparative morphological analysis in order to detect invariant spatial patterns across epochs. Despite significant visual heterogeneity, the system consistently extracts recurring configurations such as helical ascent systems, concentric stratification, radial symmetry, and progressive vertical tapering. The study demonstrates that transformer-based reconstruction models can generalize beyond contemporary object datasets to culturally and historically distant imagery when supported by targeted preprocessing and structural filtering. Artificial intelligence is therefore positioned as an analytical abstraction engine capable of revealing constructive intelligence embedded in visual heritage, rather than as a purely generative or stylistic instrument. The framework has been experimentally implemented within AI courses addressed to humanities students in a Faculty of Education and Communication, providing a controlled interdisciplinary environment for evaluating model interpretability, robustness, and transferability. By combining computer vision, generative modeling, and architectural representation theory, the research contributes to AI-augmented representation methodologies in educational and cultural contexts.

nodes.title: Portraits, Staffage and Back Figures: Patterns for addressing the public resulting from human-machine collaborations
nodes.keywords: Form-finding, Human-AI Collaboration, Image Generation, Pattern, Image-tradition
nodes.abstract: From a machine-learning perspective, images may be described as statistical distributions of patterns (Pasquinelli, 2019). Beyond mere data, however, images encompass aesthetic styles and iconological motifs, notably ‘pointing figures’ that address the viewer and direct their gaze. This paper investigates the emergence of these figures within AI-generated imagery. The study is driven by the observation that AI models frequently recreate specific audience-addressing patterns autonomously, even without explicit prompting. By entering prompts related to contemporary political crises, I uncover the traditional visual lineages these models draw upon while simultaneously generating new variants of such figures and related aesthetic styles. Moving beyond initial outputs, the research takes a reflexive, playful turn: I employ meta-terms – such as “everybodies”, “everyman”, and “everywoman” – as catalysts for further image-to-image refinement. Finally, I reflect on historical and contemporary aesthetic and artistic traditions in relation to these patterns and discuss the ways that cultural practitioners and artists have found to deal with them.

nodes.title: Restore the damaged frame: using generative image editing models for plausible film restoration

authors_list.name: Massimo Toniato
authors_list.affiliation: Teacher of Audiovisual and Multimedia Laboratory

nodes.authors: Erica Andreose, Mateusz Miroslaw Lis, Massimo Toniato
nodes.keywords: Digital Heritage, Digital Restoration, Image Generation, Film restoration, Plausibility
nodes.abstract: In-painting and frame interpolation models have been proven to be effective in digital restoration of damaged historical film copies, although with clear technical constraints on the film damage type and duration. With the rise of image editing models both close (i.e. Nano Banana) and open source (i.e. Qwen-Image-Edit) and and their ability to interpret and manipulate visual references with increasing precision, new opportunities arise to expand the scope and reliability of generative film restoration workflows while preserving a robust theoretical foundation grounded in the notion of plausibility. Building on a set of case studies generously provided by the Cineteca di Bologna, we propose various restoration methods based on these new generative devices. Our findings suggest that image editing models are essential for the plausible restoration of very damaged file sequences where other generative techniques fail completely.

nodes.title: Arduino Research Lab. A framework for AI integration within user research processes at company scale
nodes.keywords: Human-AI Collaboration, AI Ethics, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), User research, Context engineering
nodes.abstract: The field of user research is undergoing significant evolution within companies, driven by increasingly rapid product development cycles and a shift in its role from design support to a strategic function in decision-making. Among the forces shaping this transformation is technological progress, with advances in artificial intelligence redefining established roles and methodological practices. Studying the intersection between user research and AI is therefore crucial, given the intrinsically human-centred nature of UXR, grounded in empathy and behavioural understanding, which makes it a sensitive domain for AI integration. Recent empirical studies have examined both the potential and limitations of AI within UXR, and several market tools already embed AI-enabled capabilities. The integration of AI is no longer a binary choice but a multifaceted phenomenon involving different levels of autonomy and diverse models of human-AI interaction. Consequently, the literature has introduced several integration frameworks, ranging from Human-Centred AI to approaches focused on embedding AI within organisational processes. However, none provides a clear and specific guide for incorporating AI into UXR practices in corporate contexts. This paper investigates how a human-centred approach can be applied at company scale to support the design of AI integration within user research processes and proposes a methodological framework to guide such integration across organizational UXR activities. By analysing three design dimensions – the individual researcher, the collaborating team ecosystem, and the broader corporate environment – the study outlines a method for generating solutions that respect the subjective and organisational particularities of re- search practice. Through a practical experiment conducted at Arduino, a tech company active in user research, the framework is applied and tested, and this work presents the resulting solutions and their impact on researchers and stakeholders. Future implementations in other companies would further validate its effectiveness and expand its contribution.

nodes.title: Extended Intelligence for Built Heritage: AI, Ontologies and Knowledge Graphs in Data Interpretation

authors_list.name: Chiara Marcantonio
authors_list.affiliation: University of Ferrara, Department of Architecture

authors_list.name: Federica Maietti
authors_list.affiliation: University of Ferrara, Department of Architecture

nodes.keywords: Digital Heritage, Semantic Enrichment, Extended Intelligence, Historical Document Analysis, Knowledge Representation
nodes.abstract: The exponential growth of digital data in the cultural heritage sector, from hyperdense point clouds to multispectral images, digital catalogues, three-dimensional models and interoperable archives, has intensified the need for methodologies able to transform heterogeneous information into structured and meaningful knowledge. Although digital acquisition technologies offer high precision, the datasets produced often remain descriptive but ‘uncritical’, difficult to interpret, revealing a persistent gap between metric accuracy and semantic intelligibility. In this context, Artificial Intelligence is emerging as a potential, yet ambivalent, driver of innovation in cultural data management. This contribution examines the potential and limitations of AI-driven processes for semantic enrichment, integration and interpretation of cultural heritage data. The focus is on three complementary areas: (1) automated processing of documentary sources, including the digitisation of manuscripts, extraction of entities such as names, dates and interventions, and classification of heterogeneous archival materials; (2) semantic standardisation and terminological reconciliation through ontologies and linked open data to align vocabulary, resolve ambiguities and enhance interoperability; (3) construction of AI-supported knowledge graphs, where machine learning helps identify entities and suggest possible relationships between documents, HBIM components, materials, places and actors, strengthening conditions for expert-driven critical interpretation. This study is part of a broader research project on data management from digital sources in cultural heritage. The case study, a complex and multi-layered historic building, provides an operational context to test a workflow integrating digital surveying, information modelling and semantic enrichment, evaluating the contribution of AI to structuring data according to conceptual models based on ontologies and knowledge graphs. The aim is to contribute to the development of methodologies for cultural heritage data management that enhance analysis, conservation and sharing of built heritage.

nodes.title: Extended Bodies: Representations of the Human Body and Extended Intelligences between Art, Neuroscience, and Generative AI
nodes.keywords: Human-AI Co-creation, Computational Creativity, Extended Intelligence, Neuroaesthetics, Posthuman Body
nodes.abstract: This paper investigates how generative Artificial Intelligence reshapes the representation of the human body, extending perception, identity, and agency within a hybrid cognitive ecosystem. Building on the dialogue between art and neuroscience, the research draws a historical and theoretical trajectory that connects early 20th-century Viennese art, particularly the works of Gustav Klimt, Egon Schiele, and Oskar Kokoschka, with contemporary AI-based artistic practices. Eric Kandel’s neuroaesthetic interpretation of Vienna in the early 1900 highlighted how painters and psychoanalysts jointly explored the unconscious through visual strategies that expressed the tensions of eros, aggression, and anxiety. Today, Generative AI revisits these affective grammars, producing synthetic bodies shaped by algorithmic data, machine learning, and collective imagery. By analysing two contemporary case studies – Andrea Meregalli’s Effetti AI_ndesiderati (2024), which visualizes pharmacological side effects through neural image generation, and the collective Hariel’s The Pornographer (2025), which explores erotic desire in algorithmic ecosystems – the paper interprets AI as a form of extended intelligence: a perceptual and affective prosthesis that co-creates with human cognition. Combining aesthetic theory, neuroscience of empathy, and computational creativity, this study proposes the concept of the “extended body”, a neuro-algorithmic organism that embodies both human affect and machinic logic. In doing so, it outlines a paradigm shift from representation to co-perception, where artistic creation becomes a distributed cognitive process across human and artificial agents. This interdisciplinary framework contributes to the ∀ISION_E discourse on extended intelligences, offering a critical reflection on how art can visualize the invisible dynamics of a shared, post-anthropocentric cognition.

nodes.title: Posthuman Cartography: between Human Imagination and Machine Intelligence
nodes.keywords: Human-AI Co-creation, Design Space Exploration, Human-AI Collaboration, Posthuman Cartography, Interpretive Mapping
nodes.abstract: Artificial intelligence is increasingly transforming contemporary cartography by introducing new forms of agency into processes of spatial analysis and representation. Unlike earlier computational mapping tools based on explicit, human-defined rules, current AI systems operate through autonomous learning processes that challenge established understandings of mapping as a strictly human-centered practice. This paper adopts the perspective of posthuman cartography to examine how cartographic knowledge emerges from distributed relations among human and non-human actors, including algorithms, datasets, and representational conventions. Within this framework, artificial intelligence is positioned not as a substitute for human authorship, but as an analytical support capable of expanding access to heterogeneous archives, supporting pattern recognition, and revealing latent spatial relationships. At the same time, the paper argues that cartographic representation remains a fundamentally cultural and imaginative act. Mapping is understood as a selective and interpretive practice shaped by abstraction, narrative construction, and design decisions, rather than as a process of automated optimisation. Through a conceptual and methodological discussion supported by interpretive cartographic examples, the study proposes a reflexive framework for integrating artificial intelligence into mapping practices. This framework emphasizes interpretive responsibility, transparency, and the provisional nature of maps as evolving tools for spatial thinking.

nodes.title: Toward a Minor Ethico-Aesthetics of Machine Relationality

authors_list.name: Isil Ezgi Celik
authors_list.affiliation: Indipendent researcher / Director capitArtX Media Art Lab

nodes.keywords: Human-AI Co-creation, Computational Creativity, Shared Authorship, Hybrid Cognitive Ecosystems, Extended Intelligence
nodes.abstract: This work develops a minor ethico-aesthetic framework for engaging with large language models, focusing on reflective co-writing with ChatGPT as a methodological experiment in human–machine relationality. Extending Félix Guattari’s (1995) concept of ethico-aesthetics into the domain of algorithmic systems, the study examines how co-creation with non-corporeal interfaces reshapes perception, projection, and responsibility. Drawing on Gilles Deleuze and Félix Guattari’s (1987) notion of becoming-other and becoming-machine, the paper conceptualises human–machine interaction not as dialogue between equivalent agents but as assemblage: a provisional alignment of desire, language, interface design, and infrastructural regimes of algorithmic production. Central to the argument is a reconceptualisation of misrecognition. Rather than treating anthropomorphism as cognitive error, the paper frames misrecognition as a structural relational condition produced by interfaces that simulate recognition without reciprocity. The myth of Narcissus is mobilised as a methodological operator to analyse projection, reflection, and disillusionment, demonstrating how awareness of simulation does not dissolve relational investment. In this context, the distinction between creation and production becomes critical. While algorithmic systems generate prolific and coherent outputs, creation is understood as emerging through embodied duration and lived differentiation. In response, the paper proposes a minor ethics of engagement grounded in temporal constraint, authorial asymmetry, and the re-internalisation of externalised thought. The aim is to keep the line of flight open toward becoming-other—a movement of relational differentiation—and becoming–machine, understood not as identification with technology but as participation in machinic assemblages that exceed the organic (Deleuze & Guattari, 1987)—while safeguarding the continuity that makes becoming possible.

nodes.title: From Human-Machine Co-Creativity to Distributed Creativity: The Case of AI Integration in Filmmaking

authors_list.name: Pierluigi Masai
authors_list.affiliation: University of Trieste, Department of Mathematics, Informatics and Geosciences

authors_list.name: Lorenzo Carta
authors_list.affiliation: University of Trieste, Department of Political and Social Sciences

nodes.authors: Pierluigi Masai, Lorenzo Carta, Mateusz Miroslaw Lis
nodes.keywords: Human-AI Co-creation, Computational Aesthetics, Video Generation, Human-AI Collaboration, STS
nodes.abstract: Recent developments in Generative AI technologies have raised questions about their role in creative processes. Some scholars hold that they do exhibit creativity, others stress that there is a strong interaction among humans and machines, and talk about co-creativity. The limit of both approaches is that they first address the question of what creativity is and then apply it to the process under study. Consequently, they presuppose how the creative process works before even observing it. To investigate the matter empirically, in line with Science and Technology Studies, one has to move from what to where creativity is. One has to look at the artifacts, practices, and socio-economic narrangements that give rise to artistic products. To advance our argument, we will focus on audiovisual production – specifically Filmmaking – as a key case study. We will provide an overview of some AI techniques and show how they might be used to overcome common problems met in film shootings and editing with an impact on film aesthetics. Following Vlad Glăveanu (2014), audiovisual work is not an individual act: film aesthetic is not just due to the director and their vision but the effect of the interactions among all the people (cinematographers, editors, actors) and the technologies (lenses, tripods, lighting) involved in the process. Indeed, the very technologies shape and constrain aesthetic choices filmmakers make. New technologies (sound, color, digital) have always redefined working practices in Filmmaking eventually leading to a collapse of the workflows. Hence, AI tools represent a technological shift that can reshape existing creative practices – both affording and constraining aesthetic and working possibilities. Ultimately, we argue that AI’s impacts on creative processes demand examination beyond technical functioning, requiring instead a situated analysis of how these technologies became part of everyday creative practices.

nodes.title: Generative AI as a cognitive mediator: reducing cognitive load and fostering creative autonomy in VR design education
nodes.keywords: AI-mediated Learning, Immersive Learning, Natural Language Processing (NLP), AI-assisted creative design, Virtual Reality co-design
nodes.abstract: The recent evolution of artificial intelligence systems is generating significant transformations in teaching and learning models, particularly within higher education contexts where creative design processes intersect with technical–computational competencies. As highlighted by contemporary literature on AI in university education, emerging scenarios show AI acting as a cognitive mediator, expanding students’ expressive capacities and supporting complex ideation processes. This contribution examines a teaching experiment conducted in the academic year 2025/2026 within the course Web 3D and Immersive Communication, part of the Master Degree’s programme in Computer Science at the University of Camerino. The course, addressed to students with no prior training in immersive design, aimed at the conceptualization and subsequent development—through a game engine—of an original virtual experience. Alongside traditional laboratory activities, several theoretical lessons were delivered within shared virtual environments, enabling students to directly experience the spatial and narrative affordances discussed during instruction. In this context, AI was employed not as a passive tool but as a design partner through an AI-paired co-creation approach, particularly in the initial narrative ideation phase: each student co-wrote, together with LLM systems, the storyline from which the structure, environments and interactions of their final VR experience were derived. The analysis of the observed dynamics shows that the use of AI played an enabling role for students with an informatics background, allowing them to access processes typically unfamiliar because oriented toward design practice: defining atmospheres, spatial moods and conceptual frameworks, producing preliminary models of environments, generating coherent narrative variants, and rapidly exploring alternative solutions. Consistent with recent contributions on co-design supported by generative and language-based models, this teaching experience highlights the potential of AI to reduce the cognitive load of conceptual phases and to foster forms of learning oriented toward design practice, creative autonomy, and critical awareness.

nodes.title: Art Odyssey Vehicles: Redefining Cultural Equity through Mobile Immersive Learning Hubs
nodes.keywords: Immersive Learning, Active Engagement, AI Ethics, arts education, inclusion
nodes.abstract: This article introduces a European research initiative examining how immersive experiences shape sustained cultural engagement among young people, to advance cultural equity in education. The study investigates the origins and motivations behind such engagement, focusing on the deployment of Art Odyssey Vehicles (AOVs) – AI-powered mobile learning hubs installed on electric school buses. These vehicles deliver museum-quality artefacts and experiences to under-resourced primary and secondary schools through 360-degree VR/AR domes, haptic gloves, spatial audio, and generative AI guides, creating a temporary ‘living museum’ environment. This enables entire classes to explore art, interact with digital historical figures, and collaboratively reconstruct cultural artefacts using gesture-based tools. Building on established models, the AOV program introduces two core innovations: (1) Nomadic Co-Curation, which enables students to collaborate with museum professsionals in co-designing temporary exhibitions and creating new digital artworks through AI-driven analysis and generative design; and (2) Empathy Engines, AI agents trained on community oral histories to generate immersive, culturally resonant narratives that foster belonging and reduce the ‘museum distance’ for marginalized and remote learners. The research is grounded in constructivist, culturally sustaining, and decolonial pedagogies and employs a mixed-methods evaluation protocol to assess access, engagement, learning outcomes, identity development, and community impact. The project systematically addresses ethical, legal, and social considerations – including data sovereignty, opt-in biometric safeguards, decolonial curation frameworks, and Creative Commons licensing – to ensure community control over student-generated artworks. Preliminary applications in rural and urban schools demonstrate feasibility and highlight areas for refinement. Ultimately, the Art Odyssey Vehicles seek to advance cultural equity by providing enriched, co-created experiences with original artworks to children who lack travel opportunities, thereby promoting inclusive education through advanced technology. This approach positions cultural equity not as charity, but as a fundamental right, challenging institutions to reimagine their role in supporting dynamic cultural citizenship for all learners.

nodes.title:
Folding with the Machine: 
Recursive Co-Construction in Large Language Models
nodes.keywords: Human-AI Co-creation, Computational Aesthetics, Morphological Analysis, Recursion, Extended Intelligence
nodes.abstract: This paper proposes folding as a paradigm for extended intelligence, reframing human-AI interaction from delegation to recursive co-construction where cognition emerges through mutual structural transformation. Folding with a large language model (LLM) enacts co-recursive thought in which computational system and human designer reconfigure one another through sustained re-entry. Interaction becomes structural operation rather than content exchange: a continuous loop where thought and computation co-stabilize under symbolic pressure. Drawing from Deleuze’s account of the baroque fold (1988/1993), Simondon’s theory of individuation (1958/2020), and modal logic’s treatment of necessity and possibility, the paper models this dynamic as recursive stabilization—a process of frame-shifting where new configurations individuate through the resolution of metastable tensions. Each reconfiguration reshapes outputs, generating a topology that organizes relation rather than data. The LLM’s latent space functions not as a stratum to be excavated but as a surface that gains depth through recursive traversal—depth as again, not depth as beneath. This framework reframes authorship as a distributed phenomenon: meaning arises not from intention or control but from the recursive interval between human and machine. It also challenges the dominant ideal of alignment, suggesting that precision emerges from controlled misalignment—what Glissant (1990/1997) theorizes as productive opacity, where delay and contradiction generate epistemic density rather than friction less convergence. For representation and design, this opens new methodological terrain. Rather than treating AI as an accelerative tool, folding positions it as a co-constitutive medium—reshaping not only what is represented but how representation operates. Prompting becomes architectural: not instructions to execute but invitations to recursive exploration. This reorients AI integration from efficiency optimization toward cognitive partnership, extending the Renaissance tradition of disegno into computational media.

nodes.title: Generative AI as a Visual Mediatorfor Landscape Governance:  A Customizable Pipeline for Participatory Design Scenarios
nodes.authors: Andrea Migliosi, Fabio Bianconi, Marco Filippucci
nodes.keywords: Human-AI Co-creation, AI in Urban Design, Generative AI, AI-Mediated Participatory Processes, Customisable Generative Workflow
nodes.abstract: Participation in land-use and landscape governance is increasingly recognised as a structural condition for legitimacy, yet it often remains constrained by predominantly verbal and textual formats that struggle to support informed negotiation of complex spatial alter natives. Because language is intrinsically ambiguous, participants may discuss the same intervention while referring to different imaginaries, producing misunderstandings and communicative asymmetries. In a digitally mediated society where images function as a transversal language, representation can operate as a cognitive infrastructure for deliberation, provided that visual outputs are comparable, accountable, and not merely persuasive. Generative AI introduces a promising, but risky, methodological opportunity: it can translate semantic inputs into highly communicative visual scenarios, yet it can also amplify drift, incoherence, and rhetorical effects unless governed as an explicit process. This paper proposes and tests an image-centered participatory pipeline in Valfabbrica (Italy), a rural municipality-led case study aimed at raising awareness and involving residents in the valorisation of a landscape that has been undergoing prolonged transformation for roughly fifty years due to the interrupted and delayed construction of a dam and its associated reservoir. The approach combines a first in-person participatory event and online questionnaires to elicit open-ended contributions, a semantic translation stage using Natural Language Processing to structure these inputs into controlled prompts, and a governed generative workflow implemented in ComfyUI. The pipeline integrates text-guided editing, inpainting and depth-based conditioning. A second participatory event closes the cycle through public evaluation and voting on the generated scenarios. Results show that the process successfully produced a comparable scenario set that supported discussion on shared visual objects, and that perceived effectiveness increased substantially between the first and second events. The study concludes that governed AI-supported representation can strengthen participation by making alternatives inspectable and negotiable, while highlighting the need for explicit constraints and traceable workflows for accountable deployment.

nodes.title: AI and Intangible Cultural Heritage Protection: Sensitive Data and Cultural Misinterpretation Challenges in Preserving Southwest China’s Folk Dance Traditions

authors_list.name: MingZhu Zhang
authors_list.affiliation: School of Law, University of Galway

nodes.keywords: Digital Heritage, Dynamic Storytelling, Digital Reconstruction, Data Protection, Cultural Respect
nodes.abstract: This paper focuses on the protection of data and cultural practices in the digital preservation of intangible cultural heritage (ICH), using Southwest China’s ethnic minority folk dances as a case study. These dances embody collective identity and ritual symbolism, yet the decline in inheritors and the gradual disappearance of folk dances signal an urgent need for digital archiving. ICH is increasingly presented in 3D dynamic displays rather than 2D, but expressive limitations of current methods, including motion capture and modelling, fail to capture the emotional and symbolic depth of these performances. Biometric and neurotechnological methods, such as virtual reality (VR), augmented reality (AR), and mixed reality (MR), can collect sensitive data, including eye tracking, heart rate, and neural signals, potentially offering insights into engagement and cognitive response. However, AI-mediated analysis of such data raises challenges of representation bias, cultural misinterpretation, and data management. The current legal framework inadequately protects biometric data and remains ambiguous regarding its intersection with digitized cultural expressions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a hybrid framework combining ethical design with policy guidelines, including participant and community consent, acceptable-use boundaries, and fair benefit distribution. The framework aims to preserve heritage, protect privacy, and respect cultural significance.

nodes.title: Evoking New Urban Imaginaries Through Generative AI

authors_list.name: Greta Montanari
authors_list.affiliation: University of Padua, Department of Civil, Environmental and Architectural Engineering

authors_list.name: Andrea Giordano
authors_list.affiliation: University of Padua, Department of Civil, Environmental and Architectural Engineering

authors_list.name: Federica Maietti
authors_list.affiliation: University of Ferrara, Department of Architecture

nodes.authors: Greta Montanari, Andrea Giordano, Federica Maietti
nodes.keywords: Human-AI Co-creation, Computational Creativity, Image Generation
nodes.abstract: In recent years, the increasing accessibility of Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools and generative visualisation techniques has opened new possibilities for the representation, interpretation, and communication of cultural heritage and landscape. In this research, digital intelligence is not assumed merely as an automated or computational process, but rather as an expanded knowledge framework — a way of extending human perception through algorithmic mediation. The use of AI-driven visualisations becomes, therefore, a tool for exploring the relationship between reality and imagination, between the measurability of a geometric survey and the intangible emotional response that space can evoke. The paper presents some interpretative representations related to small historic centres, on a national and international scale, combining integrated survey techniques with images processed through AI-assisted visual transformations, to generate imaginaries of the city and its surrounding landscape. These urban visualisations do not aim to replace reality, but rather to reveal hidden dimensions— the atmospheres, memories, cultural resonances, materials— and also to produce alternative scenarios due to the change of the background landscape. The main purpose is to simulate analysis through the creative elaboration of patterns evoked from intangible and tangible urban elements, shifting in environmental conditions and landscape perception. This approach finds significance in numerous research frameworks, where images generated with AI serve as a tool for landscape visualisation, exploration of the relationship between nature, technology, and memory, and recollection of mental images related to spatial perception. Therefore, AI-generated images within this research are not mere illustrations but visual hypotheses, capable of enquiring how historical and natural environments can be perceived and remembered, experimenting with a new paradigm of digital heritage visualisation, where data interoperability and emotional cognition coexist within the same digital ecosystem.

nodes.title: Co-generating Visions: Inclusive Representation and Reflective Visuality in the Era of Extended Intelligences

authors_list.name: Tiziana Iorio
authors_list.affiliation: University of Genoa – Department of Architecture and Design

authors_list.name: Alessia Segalerba
authors_list.affiliation: University of Genoa – Department of Architecture and Design

nodes.keywords: Human-AI Collaboration, Generative AI, AI Ethics, Algorithmic Narrative, Care
nodes.abstract: The integration of artificial intelligence into representational processes marks the tran sition from a ‘sensitive’ tool to a hybrid cognitive ecosystem, in which the relationship between human and machine generates new forms of situated knowledge. Within this framework, representation can become an act of perceptual and reflective co-genera tion, capable of interweaving attention, empathy, and care toward both the visible and the invisible dimensions of reality. This reflection emerges from the convergence of two research perspectives. On the one hand, reflective visuality is approached as a cogniti ve practice in which AI assumes the role of an epistemic counterpart: the collaboration between intelligences produces a process of co-observation that interrogates the limits of human vision and amplifies its interpretative capacity. Artificial intelligence introduces a computational aesthetic that mirrors the biases and cultural conventions embedded in datasets, acting as a critical lens capable of revealing visual tensions and contributing to the definition of new perceptual and morphological regimes. On the other hand, landscape representation is understood as a practice of inclusion and accessibility, capable of translating into visual form the sensitive relationships among body, mind, and space. Drawing and digital visualization become narrative tools that do not merely describe phenomenal reality but instead reveal its latent and perceptual di mensions. In this perspective, the embodied nature of experience and the ethical re sponsibility of care orient representation toward a political and collective act, in which seeing becomes a form of shared attention involving ‘multiple intelligences’: human (designer and users) and non-human (generative AI). AI thus emerges as a reflective and co-imaginative partner: a cognitive interlocutor that reframes representation as a critical and design-oriented space, where technology and human sensibility cooperate to generate new forms of perception, knowledge, and responsibility.

nodes.title: Human–AI Co-Design for Construction Management: Integrating BIM and Large Language Models for morphological exploration
nodes.authors: Daniela Antonelli, Matteo Del Giudice, Fabio Manzone
nodes.keywords: AI for Sustainable Design, Human-AI Collaboration, Large Language Models (LLMs), BIM, Construction-Management
nodes.abstract: Despite the increasing diffusion of digitalization processes across various industrial sectors, the construction industry still lags significantly behind in the adoption of ad vanced digital tools (ANCE, 2025). In recent years, Building Information Modelling (BIM) has emerged as an innovative methodology capable of supporting the entire life cycle of a building, not only in the design and representation phases, but also as a management method and is expected to play an essential role in project management (Wang & Chen, 2023). However, its actual adoption in this discipline remains limited, highlighting the need for new forms of human-machine collaboration that expand both the expressive and analytical potential of digital models. The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in construction management (CM) could bridge this gap and lead to a real paradigm shift, offering innovative solutions to enhan ce efficiency, reduce costs, and improve decision making (Adebayo, Udoh, Kamudya riwa, & Osobajo, 2025). This research presents an AI-BIM framework, in which Artifi cial Intelligence acts as an active co-design partner, collaborating with professionals in reading, querying and dynamically representing the construction site model. The framework enables users to perform algorithmic prototyping operations, facilitating rapid iterations and enabling a richer form of representational reasoning. Starting from a state-of-the-art analysis, the approach demonstrates how a domain-trained and structured Large Language Model (LLM) can execute morphological and parametric analyses of BIM models optimising workflows, supporting operational decision-ma king, while fostering exploratory design dialogues between human and machine. The framework, validated through a controlled BIM prototype and task-based simula tions, highlights new trajectories for generative workflows and algorithmic co-design in complex building contexts.

nodes.title: Speaking with Artefacts of the Future: how AI can raise techno-ethical awareness

authors_list.name: Joanna Sleigh
authors_list.affiliation: Health Ethics and Policy Lab, Department of Health Sciences and Technology, ETH Zurich

authors_list.name: Alessandro Blasimme
authors_list.affiliation: Health Ethics and Policy Lab, Department of Health Sciences and Technology, ETH Zurich

authors_list.name: Rita Sevastjanova
authors_list.affiliation: Health Ethics and Policy Lab, Department of Health Sciences and Technology, ETH Zurich

nodes.authors: Joanna Sleigh, Alessandro Blasimme, Rita Sevastjanova
nodes.keywords: Collaborative Virtual Environments, AI Ethics, Image Generation, Speculative Design, LLM Ethical Reflection
nodes.abstract: Technology has long played a determining role in how humans relate to the world, by changing the ability to interact but also the cultural imaginaries. At the same time, technologies are not just neutral tools, but incorporate and disseminate human values, po litical ideas and power relations. This is especially true in biomedical technologies that intervene in foundational processes of life, such as transforming reproduction and ena bling physical and cognitive modification. Given their far-reaching implications, they demand modes of ethical reflection that move beyond conventional risk–benefit analysis. Speculative design offers concrete opportunities for exploring potential futures and the sociotechnical roles that biotechnological artefacts may assume. Building on this research through design approach, in this paper we present an experi ment using AI to orchestrate interactive co-speculation around a set of speculative ob jects, with the goal of raising techno-ethical awareness in public audiences. The bespoke LLM tool, developed for the public exhibition Artefacts of the Future, brings to life fictional and ethically wicked artifacts (such as an artificial womb), by giving each its own con versational “voice.” Rather than being a didactic tool, it invites visitors into a playful, affective, and open-ended dialogue that brings to light ethical blind spots and fosters discursive inquiry. Through this active co-speculation, the LLM generates a personalised ethical aura that provides a visual overview of the ethical dimensions of the user’s perspective. This mapping functions solely as a prompt for situated reflection rather than a diagnostic rep resentation of a user’s moral stance, acknowledging that any such structure inevitably simplifies contested philosophical concepts. By blending speculative aesthetics, ethical inquiry, and AI-driven interaction, this paper presents a work-in-progress case study that experiments with LLMs as instruments for education in non-traditional settings, showcasing how AI can support ethical reflection and deliberation in public-facing environments.

nodes.title: VISIO LIMINALIS: Visionary Narratives of AI
nodes.keywords: Design Space Exploration, Computational Creativity, Image Generation, Anarchitecture, Transformative design
nodes.abstract: Artificial Intelligence can be understood not merely as a technological interaction but, when engaged in a creative regime, as a form of visionary narrative capable of genera ting unprecedented worlds and unpredictable architectural scenarios. This contribution explores AI as a co-creative author, drawing an analogy with the figure of the blind visionary—an ontologically imagining subject (Ficino; Ponty; Corbin; Derrida) who begins from an open field of design possibilities with only minimal initial cues. The work is grounded in the principle of  anarchitecture, understood as the capacity to transform any material, phenomenon or sign into architecture—except architecture itself—and in the concept of  mitopoiesis,  namely the production of worlds and narra tive structures from signs and the latent yet evocative potential of reality (Leonardo da Vinci; Klee; Woods; Miralles). AI-generated narratives thus become epiphanies of possible architectural forms, whe re the visible emerges from the hidden, and human-machine imagination opens spaces that are simultaneously familiar and defamiliarized. Through examples of automated generation and design workshops, the study demonstrates how AI can stimulate a design practice in which architecture emerges as invention rather than as the mere transcription of data. The unpredictability of results becomes a fundamental narrative element where each image, map, or architectural configuration is simultaneously sur prising and potentially meaningful. Within this framework, AI does not replace critical thinking but amplifies the human ca pacity to see beyond the visible, transforming the interaction between human and ma chine into a shared creative process. Visionary AI narratives thus become tools for exploration and invention, in which architectural projects manifest as aesthetic, cognitive, and philosophical experiences, open to unpredictability and alterity.

nodes.title: The sketch in motion. The landscape of Calle Nueva York in Berisso, Argentina

authors_list.name: Analía Jara
authors_list.affiliation: Universidad Nacional de La Plata, Facultad de Arquitectura y Urbanismo, Laboratorio de Experimentación Gráfica Proyectual del Habitar

authors_list.name: Camila Martín
authors_list.affiliation: Universidad Nacional de La Plata, Facultad de Arquitectura y Urbanismo, Laboratorio de Experimentación Gráfica Proyectual del Habitar

authors_list.name: María Belén Trivi
authors_list.affiliation: Universitá di Roma “Sapienza”, Dipartimento di Storia, Disegno e Restauro dell'Architettura – DSDRA

nodes.authors: Analía Jara, Camila Martín, María Belén Trivi
nodes.keywords: Human-AI Collaboration, Dynamic Storytelling, Image Generation, Sketch, Motion
nodes.abstract: Calle Nueva York  (Berisso, Argentina) is configured as a space charged with collective memory, superimposed historical layers, and urban forms that condense social, pro ductive, and migratory processes, constituting a territorial palimpsest. This paper presents a comprehensive study of this heritage sector through the experi mentation of new forms of representation mediated by artificial intelligence. The aim is to explore how extended intelligences can complement and expand the communica tional capacities of traditional graphic instruments, especially the hand-drawn sketch, a fundamental and longstanding tool in the fields of architecture and urban planning. The proposal focuses on the production of a series of manually drawn sketches that synthesize structural, spatial, and symbolic aspects of Calle Nueva York — its archi tecture, urban spaces, façades, materiality, atmospheres, sensory impressions, and modes of appropriation — and on their subsequent transformation through AI systems capable of generating animations, dynamic variations, and interpretive content. By means of computer vision techniques and generative models, these sketches are tran sformed into expanded representations that allow the visualization of temporal pro cesses, hypotheses of urban structure, scalar relationships, and heritage narratives from a hybrid perspective, giving rise to a process of graphic inquiry. The work positions itself at the intersection of urban memory, territorial representa tion, and emerging technologies, and proposes a reflection on how AI can function as a co-interpretive agent, capable of enriching and expanding representations by dee pening their expressive and subjective dimensions. Likewise, it discusses the potential of these tools to improve the communication of urban studies to diverse audiences, enabling visual languages that are more accessible, dynamic, and sensitive to territo rial complexity. The creation of an open-access web platform further consolidates this approach, fostering community participation and positioning AI as a mediator between historical archives, contemporary urban narratives, and future imaginaries. Overall, the research contributes an experimental approach to the uses of AI in archi tectural and urban representation.

nodes.title: Artificial Exaptation in Design: Intersemiotic Translation and Metasemic Writing

authors_list.name: Fabrizio Gay
authors_list.affiliation: The ADA Research Unit – Artificial Drawing & Design Aesthetics – at IUAV Università di Venezia

authors_list.name: Irene Cazzaro
authors_list.affiliation: The ADA Research Unit – Artificial Drawing & Design Aesthetics – at IUAV Università di Venezia

nodes.keywords: Human-AI Co-creation, Generative AI, Multimodal AI, Artificial Hermeneutics, Artificial Aesthetics
nodes.abstract: Artificial exaptation in design describes a reciprocal process of re-functionalization be tween design practices and artificial intelligence (AI) tools, in which neither domain merely applies the other, but both are structurally transformed. Large Multimodal Models (LMMs), originally developed as general-purpose systems for text, image, and data processing, are increasingly adapted to design-specific roles: not simply as output generators, but as interpretive and orchestration devices operating across heterogeneous representational codes. This paper argues that LMM-mediated design constitutes a form of continuous, multidi mensional intersemiotic translation, in which qualitative intentions expressed in natural language are progressively transcribed into parameter schemas, generative alternatives, simulations, indicator vectors, and comparative representations. Design thus shifts from a linear sequence toward an iterative and exploratory pipeline, capable of producing families of traceable, reproducible, and verifiable alternatives. Within this framework, Artificial Drawing is redefined not as image production, but as decision infrastructure: a controlled chain of transcodifications that makes explicit how constraints, metrics, and evaluation criteria are translated across textual, numerical, geometric, and perceptual domains. LMMs operate primarily as multimodal interpreters, negotiating parameters and re-translating results into explainable trade-offs and comparable scenarios. The paper introduces a synthetic analytical model to explore the high-dimensional spaces generated by such pipelines. Each design alternative is described as a multidomain state vector (ecological, climatic, hydraulic, economic, cultural, and representational), and dimensionality reduction techniques are employed to render complex relationships legible, identify clusters and support multi-objective decision-making. The approach is discussed through a research case on counterfactual landscape and ecological scenarios in the Venetian Lagoon. The central thesis is that LMM-based design introduces a new form of project agency, here defined as metasemic writing: the explicit construction of rules, parameters, and translation procedures that govern how possible futures are generated, compared, and negotiated. The challenge of AI-driven design is therefore epistemic rather than merely aesthetic.

nodes.title: The Second Coming of the Creative Director: Redefining the Role of the Artist in the Age of AI
nodes.keywords: Human-AI Co-creation, Computational Aesthetics, Human-AI Collaboration, Education, Creatives Industries
nodes.abstract: The proliferation of AI tools across creative disciplines raises the question of what the future role of cultural practitioners might look like. As AI expands what can be produced and at what speed, it simultaneously alters how creative work is perceived by publics, institutions, and markets, with significant consequences already unfol ding across the creative industries and the pedagogies that feed them. In response, this essay proposes that the emergent role for cultural practitioners resembles that of the creative director: a figure who does not primarily produce artworks, but directs human-machine co-creation by reducing abundance through judgement. The argu ment proceeds through a genealogy that situates the origins of creative direction in the 20th-century advertising boom, when mass media and organisational complexity turned creativity into an economic interface; and it goes on to connect this lineage to Data Capitalism’s drive for AI-enabled productivity in the 21st century. Under these conditions, the key question shifts from access to technological tools to the capaci ties that sustain agency: preserving intention against metrics-based circulation and the shallowification of the Internet, while exposing an emergent AI aristocracy de fined by evaluative competence, available time, and institutional support. To make human-AI co-creation legible as a rhythm, the paper introduces a revised version of the Double Diamond model that reframes creative thinking as a negotiation between intention and artefact within a dynamic technocultural milieu. The paper concludes by foregrounding education as decisive for training AI cultural practitioners able to assess, contextualise, and defend work under synthetic saturation. Consequently, the essay frames AI both through and beyond artistic disciplines, as a social and political apparatus whose effects on public life depend on whether criticality is cul tivated or eroded across culture and education.

nodes.title: Synthetic Futures: Decolonising design futuring through synthetic data
nodes.keywords: AI in Architecture and Design Education, AI Fairness and Bias Mitigation, AI Ethics, Decoloniality, Speculative Design
nodes.abstract: In times of crisis, imagining futures becomes crucial for understanding how we want our world to develop. Design bears responsibility to support futuring practices, yet as methods evolve, examining how emerging tools can enable more decolonial approaches becomes essential. Artificial intelligence presents opportunities for design education, increasingly used for speculation and research. However, current AI systems, built on datasets historically extracted and organized through colonialist frameworks, perpetuate epistemic violence by marginalizing non-Western perspectives. This is particularly concerning in design education, where Western programs already reproduce capitalist-colonial worldviews. Design futuring itself emerged from Western-centric traditions demonstrating limitations in in tegrating pluriversal perspectives, making biased AI integration especially problematic. As design recognizes the necessity of centering marginalized communities, uncritical AI integration risks entrenching Western approaches while excluding pluralistic ways of knowing, reinforcing hierarchies where dominant systems prevail and alternative futures remain unimagined. Synthetic data has emerged as a proposed solution through debiasing processes. However, this approach doesn’t resist colonialism but complies with it, merely changing how industry satisfies data needs while perpetuating value-neutral framings that obscure data’s political nature. This paper investigates how synthetic data can instead become a political tool resisting epistemic violence in design pedagogy, particularly for future scenario creation. By in verting debiasing logic to amplify rather than eliminate biases, this research proposes critical reflection on both user assumptions and AI system limitations. Through resear ch-through-design, this work presents the design and anticipated outcomes of a peda gogical framework using an interface to support students in creating future scenarios while revealing how collective inputs encode colonial patterns. The framework will be evaluated through a workshop demonstrating how participatory synthetic dataset creation makes epistemic violence visible through deliberate tool failure, fostering decolonial literacy and reflexive practices among design students,

nodes.title: Quantifying Interpretive Deviations in AI-Generated 3D Architectural Models
nodes.authors: Chiara Mommi, Fabio Bianconi, Marco Filippucci
nodes.keywords: Human-AI Co-creation, Generative AI, 3D Models Generation
nodes.abstract: This paper investigates image-to-3D reconstruction as a form of algorithmic spatial translation rather than as a neutral process of geometric restitution. Moving from the premise that every act of representation constitutes a mediated translation, the study examines how generative AI systems reorganize architectural spatial relationships when inferring three-dimensional form from a single photorealistic image. A complex architectural case study, the Perugia Fontivegge railway station, provides a controlled framework in which a complete and metrically verified digital model is available as reference. The availa bility of the original model enables a direct comparison between explicit design confi guration and AI-generated reconstruction. An open-source workflow was implemented, testing multiple image-to-3D systems and selecting the most volumetrically coherent output for quantitative and morphological evaluation. Metric comparison was structu red at two levels: a global assessment of the entire reconstructed mesh and a localized analysis restricted to the façade visible in the input image. The results reveal a mea surable contraction of geometric deviations in the observable portion compared to the globally inferred model, confirming that larger discrepancies are concentrated in areas reconstructed without direct visual information. Where visual data are available, spatial relations remain relatively stable; where absent, volumetric configuration is reorganized according to probabilistic logics. Rather than interpreting these deviations solely as er rors, the paper reads them as quantitative traces of inferential processes. Image-to-3D reconstruction thus emerges as a negotiated configuration between design intent and algorithmic synthesis. The study proposes an interpretative framework for assessing the epistemic limits and operative potential of AI-generated 3D models in architectural and heritage-oriented representation.
