nodes.title: Echoes of the Oracle: Accelerating Heritage Gamification whit AI process
nodes.keywords: Gamification, Human-AI Collaboration, 3D Models Generation, Intangible Cultural Heritage, Serious Games
nodes.abstract: The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) into educational paradigms is fundamentally reshaping the preservation and dissemination of Cultural Heritage (CH). Recent scholarship emphasizes the capacity of AI-driven immersive environments to enhance perso nalized learning and foster deeper cultural awareness, particularly regarding intangible histories. However, the production of photorealistic assets for such “Serious Games” traditionally demands prohibitive time and technical resources. Addressing this bottle neck, this study presents an experimental “One-Day Sprint” methodology to construct a high-fidelity interactive environment. Leveraging Rodin AI and Hitem 3D for rapid gene rative auto-modeling, alongside AI-assisted blueprint management within Unreal Engine 5.7, we reconstructed the Sanctuary of Apollo at Delphi during the consultation of the Pythia. The project utilizes UE5’s next-generation features, specifically Nanite for Vegetation to render the outdoor dense, geometric complexity of the Mount Parnassus flo ra, and Lumen to dynamically simulate the atmospheric, mystical lighting of the adyton (inner sanctum). This first-person experience focuses on the intangible heritage of the prophetic ritual, using Text-Image to model and Natural Language Processing (NLP) to generate context-aware narrative interactions. The experiment demonstrates that Ge nerative AI tools can effectively democratize the creation of hyper-realistic educational spaces, whether fantasy or real-life, shifting the focus from technical asset modeling to

nodes.title: Giving Voice to Absent Matter: Human–Machine Narrative Practices for Endangered Mediterranean Heritage

authors_list.name: Maria Trombetta
authors_list.affiliation: "Università degli Studi Mediterranea di Reggio Calabria

nodes.keywords: Digital Heritage, Dynamic Storytelling, Human-AI Collaboration, Interactive Storytelling, Immersive Experiences
nodes.abstract: Across the Mediterranean, many heritage sites threatened by conflict, abandonment, or environmental pressures persist only as fragments or distant memories. When a place becomes inaccessible—or disappears altogether—its silence raises a question: how can we continue to narrate what is no longer materially present? This contribution explores how drawing, storytelling, and digital tools can help re-establish a relationship with en dangered heritage, especially where physical contact is impossible. The research adopts the perspective of extended intelligences, considering the interaction between human interpretation and machine outputs as a shared cognitive space. Representation is not reconstruction, but evocation: allowing traces, testimonies, and partial memories to speak again. Technology supports human imagination, questioning, and reframing of absence, while raising issues of authorship, transparency, and responsibility in visual knowledge production. The work investigates Mediterranean sites at risk and inaccessible due to conflict. Rather than offering definitive images, it experiments with narrative maps, interpretive drawings, immersive environments, and digital archives that collect stories, viewpoints, and visual reflections. The aim is to create spaces where absence becomes thinkable, discussable, and emotionally perceptible.Two design experiences shaped this approach: an augmented reality application at the National Archaeological Museum of Reggio Calabria, and a community-based AR project within the MAUA/Bepart initiative in Diamante. In both, the central outcome was the creation of shared narratives, where technology deepened connection with objects, places, and histories, rather than simply adding information. This contribution argues that safeguarding endangered heritage re quires interpretive practices combining critical sensitivity and imaginative engagement— where human and machine intelligence collaborate in giving voice to what remains, even when matter has disappeared.

nodes.title: From semantic segmentation to image-to-3D processes: a methodological framework for the extensive modeling and valorization of archival documents
nodes.keywords: Historical Document Analysis, Digital Archives, 3D Models Generation, Metric Analysis, Archive Valorisation
nodes.abstract: The research offers a methodological reflection on the use of image-to-3D processes to enhance archival documents, understood as complex memory devices that intertwine documentary, design, and interpretative dimensions. From this perspective, the archive is not considered a simple container of graphic works, but a layered system of knowle dge, visions, and cultural practices that requires advanced tools to be made accessible, interrogable, and comprehensible in its entirety. The research falls within the field of AI applied to the conservation and enhancement of cultural heritage and takes the archival system of the Pinacoteca di Brera as a case study, with reference to the materials cata loged by the Central Institute for Cataloguing and Documentation (ICCD). The objective is to define an operational framework for the three-dimensional translation of historical drawings using generative artificial intelligence systems, with particular attention to epistemological implications and criteria for evaluating geometric and stylistic accuracy.The methodology integrates a preliminary phase of archival image acquisition and normali zation with pixel-wise semantic segmentation processes based on deep learning archi tectures, aimed at recognizing and classifying figurative elements (volumes, decorative elements, structures). Semantic structuring, organized according to interoperable formats and integrated with controlled vocabularies, allows the historical image to be transformed into a relational data system, in which visual entities are associated with typological, constructional, and historical-critical metadata. The two-dimensional-three-dimensional transition, through the use of image-to-3D artificial intelligence processes, becomes a semantically informed translation, based on a dialogue between automated computation and critical mediation. In this framework, artificial intelligence is understood as an operational infrastructure for the construction of accessible and interoperable digital atlases of archival heritage, oriented towards extensive and inclusive use.

nodes.title: Drawing, AI and Built Heritage Conservation: From Metric Rigour to Perceptual Depth, the Rotonda of San Tomè Case Study

authors_list.name: Alessio Cardaci
authors_list.affiliation: Department of Engineering and Applied Sciences

authors_list.name: Pietro Azzola
authors_list.affiliation: Department of Engineering and Applied Sciences

nodes.authors: Alessio Cardaci, Antonella Versaci, Pietro Azzola
nodes.keywords: Digital Survey, AI-enhanced Photogrammetry, Geometric Analysis, Representation, Artificial Intelligence
nodes.abstract: The growing integration of surveying tools with Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems is substantially transforming the processes of knowledge, representation, and communication of built heritage, introducing both operational and epistemological shift.This paper proposes an approach that integrates metric rigour and perceptual depth, by combining controlled and verifiable 3D digital survey protocol with neural rendering techniques, specifically 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), as a perceptual interface for the data. Focusing on the case study of the Rotonda di San Tomè in Almenno San Bartolomeo, the research discusses how reality-based practices (UAS photogrammetry with nadir and oblique acquisitions, a topographic support network, integration with TLS, and quality control procedures) can provide a “measurable” basis for neural models capable of generating consistent synthetic views, visual continuity, and immersive modes of access, while preserving metric verifiability and traceability. The proposed workflow explicitly distinguishes three levels of information (measured data, interpolated data, and generated content) to prevent radiometric persuasiveness from replacing dimensional verifiability. Methodologically, the contribution describes dataset construction and export to formats compatible with neural pipelines, highlighting critical consistency requirements among calibration, resolution, intrinsic and extrinsic parameters, and point cloud quality— factors that directly affect the stability of the Gaussian representation and the reduction of artefacts. The results are discussed in terms of the complementarity between photogrammetric reconstructions (explicit and controllable geometries) and neural renderings (navigability, readability, and perceptual continuity), highlighting both potential and limitations: reproducibility, algorithmic transparency, the risk of hyper-realism, and technological obsolescence. Finally, the paper proposes operational criteria for end-to-end process traceability and for the quantitative and qualitative validation of results, orienting the use of AI towards responsible practices of documentation, conservation, and valorisation.

nodes.title: LLM-DRIVEN REPRESENTATION. An AI-Augmented Computational Workflows for CNC Fabrication
nodes.keywords: Human-AI Collaboration, Large Language Models (LLMs), Fine-Tuning Techinques, Digital manufacturing, CNC
nodes.abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) are expanding the conceptual and operational space of computational design workflows, particularly when integrated with fabrication-oriented environments. This research examines a hybrid process developed within the Computation for Fabrication research project, where an LLM is employed as a generative and interpretative agent in the early design stages and subsequently em bedded within a parametric modelling-to-fabrication pipeline. The model produces both visual and vector outputs (SVG), which are semantically structured and geometrically interpretable. Visual outputs are downsampled and pixelated, generating discretised image matrices that act as intermediate morphological representations. These matrices are decoded through a visual programming language tool within a 3D CAD environment, via custom scripts, enabling the construction of three-dimensional geometries infor med by fabrication constraints. The resulting models are translated into toolpath stra tegies for CNC milling, linking algorithmic generation and robotic materialisation within a continuous workflow. The study demonstrates, through an experimental case study of CNC-milled prototypes, that LLM-driven generation can operate as a morphological explorer, expanding the design space through controlled variation; as an interpretative system capable of identifying and describing emergent patterns; and as a proto-fabrication layer that provides machinable vector and raster-derived data. This integration reveals a co-creative human–machine process in which form, representation, and fabrication are negotiated iteratively. The findings highlight how AI-supported design can articulate new computational aesthetics, reduce the representational gap between concept and production, and establish an operative bridge between natural language, parametric modelling, and automated fabrication.

nodes.title: From Noise to Intention: “Artistic Intention” in Hybrid Autoregressive–Diffusion Pipelines and Diffusion Language Models for Drawing with AI

authors_list.name: Giovanni Rasetti
authors_list.affiliation: Università degli Studi "G. d'Annunzio" Chieti – Pescara

nodes.keywords: Human-AI Co-creation, Extended Intelligence, AI Ethics
nodes.abstract: In AI-assisted image-making, “drawing” no longer coincides with a purely manual ges ture: it unfolds across prompts, latent spaces, and iterative model interactions. This paper investigates how three generative regimes – (1) pure diffusion text-to-image, (2) hybrid pipelines that couple autoregressive large language models (LLMs) with diffusion generators, and (3) diffusion-based language models – redistribute artistic intention when humans draw with AI. Standard diffusion workflows concentrate intention into a compressed verbal specification that is handed to an opaque stochastic denoising pro cess. Hybrid AR-LLM diffusion setups mitigate this rigidity: the user co-writes prompts in dialogue with a textual agent that reformulates, expands, and normalizes idiosyn cratic ideas before they are translated into images. Diffusion language models extend this logic by applying denoising directly to text, treating prompts as semantic sketches refined through cycles of noise and clarification and aligning linguistic and visual gener ation within a shared diffusion paradigm. To interpret these shifts, the paper mobilizes Nietzsche’s notion of Dichtende Vernunft – poetic reason as the capacity for imaginative leaps irreducible to rule-based calculation–and Adorno’s concept of Ästhetische Rationalität, according to which artworks embody a non-instrumental form of rationality that resists complete conceptual capture. The central question becomes whether extended human–machine pipelines reinforce or erode this poetic and aesthetic rationality: do they merely optimize convergence toward statistically likely images, or can they support genuinely inventive departures? By framing prompts, intermediate texts, and model trajectories as moments of “extended intention,” the paper links abstract debates on au thorship and creativity to the concrete graphic products of AI-assisted drawing. It argues that the move from hybrid to fully diffusion-based language–image stacks marks a new configuration of artistic control and surprise in architectural and artistic representation.

nodes.title: Is AI biased? Some thoughts from the Cultural Heritage perspective

authors_list.name: Veronica Tronconi
authors_list.affiliation: Department of Architecture and Industrial Design, University of Campania Luigi Vanvitelli

nodes.keywords: Knowledge Representation, AI Ethics, Image Generation, Cultural Representation, Critical Discourse Analysis
nodes.abstract: In an essay on the methodologies of Critical Discourse Analysis, an approach to analyz ing how language and/or images are used in social contexts to construct, maintain, or challenge power relations and ideologies, the authors Machin and Mayr state: “the communicator always has a range of semiotic choices available to them when they wish to represent a person (or an object, nda). The choices they make will never be neutral [...]. These choices allow us to place these people in the social world and to highlight certain aspects of identity we wish to draw attention to or omit” (Machin & Mayr, 2012, p. 103). Therefore, if, according to sociological disciplines, no product of human intelligence can be ‘neutral’, but contains within itself ideologies, hierarchies, and interpretations oriented in a cultural, social, or political sense, one wonders whether the same bias es are present in generative Artificial Intelligence or whether it ultimately constitutes a neutral communicator. At the same time, the pervasiveness of artificial intelligence has now invaded the field of world Cultural Heritage, making it possible to manage and interpret millions of digitized artworks from the databases of cultural institutions around the world.  The question that the following research seeks to answer, if artificial intelligence suffers from the same logical biases that affect human intelligence, identifies the theme of the representation of world cultural heritage as a field of investigation.  Through the use of AI image generation software, the author aims to demonstrate whether the rep resentation of world cultural heritage thus created is neutral, or whether it is based on mechanisms of stereotyping, sensationalism, over- or under-representation of artifact and/or cultures, and whether each social, religious, territorial, and historical context can be accurately represented. If cultural heritage identifies us as individuals and as communities, are all individuals or communities fairly represented in the context of generative artificial intelligence?

nodes.title: Generative UI as a new vision to draw usable interfaces

authors_list.name: Elena Benedetto
authors_list.affiliation: University of Genoa – Department of Architecture and Design

nodes.keywords: Generative Design, Design Optimization, Generative AI, UX UI design, Generative UI
nodes.abstract: During the 2020 pandemic, numerous digital services were rapidly introduced, especially in the healthcare field. This accelerated adoption highlighted and amplified the digital divide, which was not driven solely by the lack of internet connectivity but also by the inadequacy of user interfaces (UIs) in supporting heterogeneous user populations (Ramsetty et al., 2020). These cases underscore the limitations of traditional UI design, which assumes that a single interface can effectively accommodate multiple user profiles with heterogeneous needs. Such standardized solutions are increasingly incompatible with the expectations and abilities of a diversified society. To address this issue, the Nielsen Norman Group proposed the use of Generative UIs (GenUIs). These interfaces employ artificial intelligence models to detect user needs and dynamically adapt to them in real time (Moran et al., 2024). However, current discussions on GenUIs often focus primarily on AI capabilities while lacking a structured design methodology capable of regulating interface generation and adaptation, this paper examines how this new vision changes the drawing of UIs by proposing a conceptual operational framework inspired by three complementary theoretical traditions: shape grammars, which provide formal systems for rule-based visual generation (Stiny et al., 1971); interface plasticity, which defines how interfaces can adapt to contextual constraints while preserving usability (Calvary et al., 2001); self-adaptive systems, which enable continuous monitoring, decision-making, and dynamic reconfiguration (De Lemos et al., 2013). These three strands were selected because they address three interdependent dimensions required for Generative UI sys tems, specifically structural coherence, contextual adaptability, and real-time dynamic generation. The aim is to demonstrate how GenUIs can offer a potential response to the digital divide by combining formal control, adaptive behavior, and AI-driven personalization within a unified workflow.

nodes.title: From Prompt to Geometry. A Critical Assessment of NLP Tools for Modeling Vaulted Systems

authors_list.name: Fabrizio Natta
authors_list.affiliation: Politecnico di Torino, Department of Architecture and Design (DAD)

authors_list.name: Andrea Tomalini
authors_list.affiliation: Politecnico di Torino, Department of Architecture and Design (DAD)

nodes.keywords: Human-AI Co-creation, Algorithmic Prototyping, Natural Language Processing (NLP), Vaulted Systems, AI-driven morphological analysis
nodes.abstract: This paper explores the genesis and theoretical framework of ‘Vibe Coding’, aa recently popularized paradigm shift in software development. This approach transitions from deterministic manual syntax to the probabilistic mediation of intent through Natural Language, repositioning the programmer as an orchestrator of agentic AI systems. In the architectural domain, this evolution is further empowered by the Model Context Protocol (MCP), which facilitates seamless interaction between AI assistants and specialized CAD environments. Conceived as an exploratory study rather than a formal methodological framework, the research evaluates the practical implications of vibe coding through a series of preliminary experimental tests focused on the automated generation of complex vaulted systems – specifically barrel, groin, and cloister vaults. To probe these novel tools, a total of 12 structured tests were conducted: 8 tests utilizing Grasshopper (where the LLM autonomously generated the visual script) and 4 tests using Rhino 8 (RhinoCommon via Python). The study analyzes the AI’s ability to translate qualitative prompts and multimodal inputs into functional parametric models. While initial tests demonstrate a significant ‘productivity unlock’ and high syntactical proficiency, increased geometric complexity reveals substantial limitations in spatial reasoning, radial symmetry, and topological consistency. The findings indicate that while Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at generating logical skeletons, they frequently produce ‘code smells’ and structural errors that necessitate expert manual intervention. The transition from purely textual to multimodal prompting is identified as a crucial factor in reducing spatial orientation errors, yet the AI still struggles with the ‘cleaning’ of geometric databases. Given the preliminary nature of these tests, the current evaluation relies predominantly on qualitative assessment, aiming to map the boundaries of these emerging workflows. Ultimately, the paper argues that vibe coding currently serves as a powerful ‘intuition amplifier’ and pedagogical tool rather than a replacement for professional expertise. It concludes by advocating for a ‘Responsible Vibe Coding’ framework to mitigate risks such as architectural debt, ensuring that the architect remains a critical validator within an increasingly automated design workflow.

nodes.title: Artificial Intelligence and Integrity: Digital Documentation of Disappearing Rural Built Heritage in UNESCO Landscapes
nodes.keywords: Digital Heritage, Large Language Models (LLMs), Fine-Tuning Techinques, AI-assisted heritage conservation, Rural vernacular architecture
nodes.abstract: Rural built heritage in inland areas of Europe is facing a persistent crisis, with vernacular structures deteriorating due to depopulation, economic decline, and climate pressures. This research addresses this challenge by developing an innovative application that uses Fine-Tuned Large Language Models (LLMs), an Artificial Intelligence (AI) model with advanced image and multimodal data analysis capabilities, to systematically document, classify, and assess existing built heritage. The application processes contemporary photographs and historical archival materials, including books and documentation, to create comprehensive digital records of structures in their current condition, establishing baseline data for informed preservation decisions. The research concentrates on two renowned UNESCO landscapes: the Dolomites mountains (UNESCO, 2009) and the Langhe-Roero-Monferrato wine regions of Italy (UNESCO, 2014). These World Heritage sites exemplify the vulnerability of rural architectural components, where alpine farmsteads, mountain refuges, and viticultural structures face abandonment and collapse. By focusing on these protected yet endangered landscapes, the study demonstrates how AI can serve preservation authorities in documenting threatened heritage before irreversible loss occurs, while supporting conservation efforts grounded in verifiable historical evidence. This research stresses the importance of early damage detection to prevent losses at later stages, and the materiality preservation achieved through AI is grounded in the Western notion of authenticity in heritage conservation. In this regard, the authors also investigate how AI-generated evaluations can support the preservation of heritage integrity and how best to benefit from AI assistance. Finally, the study proposes an AI-aided workflow, as well as an archive of built heritage that reveals how algorithms perceive and learn from heritage and can assist in preserving it. These calibrated AI models demonstrate the ability to classify heritage and to determine when to suggest restoration, based on quantifiable assessments of the existing fabric, thereby introducing methodological innovation to preservation decision-making processes.

nodes.title: AI and Representation Discipline: The REAACH Symposium Observatory
nodes.authors: Andrea Giordano, Michele Russo, Roberta Spallone
nodes.keywords: Diffusion Models (DMs), Image Generation, 3D Models Generation
nodes.abstract: The research produced within the framework of the REAACH Symposium can be seen as indicative of trends within the field of Representation, which is beginning to engage with issues, methodologies and techniques offered by AI. This paper presents a critical analysis of the various intersections between the fields of Representation and Artificial. Intelligence, beginning in the 1920s. The lens through which this investigation is conducted is that of the two main areas of interest within representation: Cultural Heritage and Innovative Design.
